The **Harvard Crimson football** program represents [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") at the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") [Division I Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (formerly Division I-AA). Harvard's football program is one of the oldest in the world, having begun competing in the sport in 1873. The Crimson has a legacy that includes 13 [national championships](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") and 20 [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame") inductees, including the first African-American college football player [William H. Lewis](/wiki/William_H._Lewis "William H. Lewis"), [Huntington "Tack" Hardwick](/wiki/Huntington_Hardwick "Huntington Hardwick"), [Barry Wood](/wiki/Barry_Wood_(American_football) "Barry Wood (American football)"), [Percy Haughton](/wiki/Percy_Haughton "Percy Haughton"), and [Eddie Mahan](/wiki/Eddie_Mahan "Eddie Mahan"). Harvard is the tenth [winningest team](/wiki/List_of_winningest_college_football_teams "List of winningest college football teams") in NCAA Division I football history.[[2]](#cite_note-FBS-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FCS-3)

The Crimson play their home games at [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of Harvard Crimson football seasons](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_Crimson_football_seasons "List of Harvard Crimson football seasons")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

Though [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") style "carrying game" with use of hands permitted (as opposed to "kicking games" where hands were not permitted) between freshmen and sophomores were played in 1858[[4]](#cite_note-4) the rugby team was not founded until December 6, 1872,[[5]](#cite_note-5) by former members of the [Oneida Football Club](/wiki/Oneida_Football_Club "Oneida Football Club"), formed in 1862 and considered by some historians as the first formal "football" club in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-geary-6)[[7]](#cite_note-allaway-7)[[8]](#cite_note-oneida1-8)[[9]](#cite_note-globe-9) Oneida had developed the "[Boston game](/wiki/Boston_game "Boston game")" (or "Boston rules)", an early code of [football](/wiki/Football "Football") that was also used by the recently established Harvard club.[[10]](#cite_note-pfra1-10) Harvard team is considered the oldest rugby team in the United States.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Old "Football Fightum" had been resurrected at Harvard in 1872, when Harvard resumed playing football. Harvard, however, had adopted a version of football which allowed carrying, albeit only when the player carrying the ball was being pursued. As a result of this, Harvard refused to attend the rules conference organized by the other schools and continued to play under its own code.

In 1873 when the Harvard team received an invitation from the [McGill University football club](/wiki/McGill_Redbirds_football "McGill Redbirds football"). The McGill team was then in a similar situation as Harvard, as they sought some team with which to play rugby football and no other club wanted to play that game. Harvard boys agreed to a rugby match with McGill under the condition the Canadians played the Boston Game. As McGill accepted, a two-game series was scheduled for May 1874 in Boston. The team captains sent letters detailing their respective game's rules and it was agreed that the first game would be played under Boston rules and the second under rugby rules.[[6]](#cite_note-geary-6)[[10]](#cite_note-pfra1-10)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Inasmuch as rugby football had been transplanted to Canada from England, the McGill team played under a set of rules which allowed a player to pick up the ball and run with it whenever he wished. Another rule, unique to McGill, was to count [tries](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)") (the act of grounding the football past the opposing team's goal line; it is important to note that there was no end zone during this time), as well as goals, in the scoring. In the rugby rules of the time, a touchdown only provided the chance to kick a free goal from the field. If the kick was missed, the touchdown did not count.

The first game (attended by nearly 500 spectators, mostly students) showed the kicking of a round ball as the most prominent feature of the "Boston Game". The Canadians were easily defeated by a Harvard squad familiarised with the Boston rules in contrast to the lack of experience of McGill players.[[15]](#cite_note-hargenes-15) During the second game under the rugby rules, the Harvard players easily adapted to the less restrictive rules of the game, such as the unlimited running and passing the ball or the more aggressive and constant tackling.[[6]](#cite_note-geary-6) Within a few years, Harvard had both adopted McGill's rules and persuaded other U.S. university teams to do the same. On June 4, 1875, Harvard played another rugby match v [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_Jumbos_football "Tufts Jumbos football") (lost 1–0),[[16]](#cite_note-harvbook-16) and then Yale on November 13. That game caused Yale to drop association football in favour of rugby.[[6]](#cite_note-geary-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/McGill_v_harvard_football_game_1874.jpg/250px-McGill_v_harvard_football_game_1874.jpg)](/wiki/File:McGill_v_harvard_football_game_1874.jpg)

A moment of the third Harvard vs. [McGill](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") game, played in October 1874

The McGill team traveled to [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts") to meet Harvard. On May 14, 1874, [the first game](/wiki/1874_Harvard_vs._McGill_football_game "1874 Harvard vs. McGill football game"), played under Harvard's rules, was won by Harvard with a score of 3–0.[[17]](#cite_note-davis-17) The next day, the two teams played under "McGill" rugby rules to a scoreless tie.[[18]](#cite_note-PFRA1-18) The games featured a round ball instead of a rugby-style oblong ball.[[17]](#cite_note-davis-17) This series of games represents an important milestone in the development of the modern game of American football.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) In October 1874, the Harvard team once again traveled to Montreal to play McGill in rugby, where they won by three tries. Harvard later brought the Harvard/McGill rules to a game against another American college. On June 4, 1875, Harvard played [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_University "Tufts University") under rules that included each side fielding 11 men, the ball was advanced by kicking or carrying it, and tackles of the ball carrier stopped play.[[21]](#cite_note-21) This is likely the first game between two American colleges in this early era that most resembled the modern game of American football.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Ralph_Horween_2.jpg/120px-Ralph_Horween_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ralph_Horween_2.jpg)

[Ralph Horween](/wiki/Ralph_Horween "Ralph Horween") played for the unbeaten Harvard Crimson football teams of 1919 and 1920

The Harvard Crimson was one of the dominant forces in the early days of intercollegiate football, winning 9 [college football national championships](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") between 1890 and 1919.[[22]](#cite_note-gocrimson1-22)[[23]](#cite_note-NCAARB-23) In the forty-year period from 1889 to 1928, Harvard had more than 80 first-team All-American selections.[[24]](#cite_note-allam-24) Under head coach [Percy Haughton](/wiki/Percy_Haughton "Percy Haughton"), Harvard had three consecutive undefeated seasons from 1912 to 1914, including two [perfect seasons](/wiki/Perfect_season "Perfect season") in 1912 and 1913.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In both 1919 and 1920, headed by [All-American](/wiki/All-American "All-American") brothers [Arnold Horween](/wiki/Arnold_Horween "Arnold Horween") and [Ralph Horween](/wiki/Ralph_Horween "Ralph Horween") (who also attended [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School")), Harvard was undefeated (9–0–1, as they outscored their competition 229–19, and 8–0–1, respectively).[[26]](#cite_note-googleusercontent1-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The team won the 1920 [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(game) "Rose Bowl (game)") against the [University of Oregon](/wiki/University_of_Oregon "University of Oregon"), 7–6.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-google1-31) It was the only bowl appearance in Harvard history.[[32]](#cite_note-nytimes1996-32)

### NCAA Division I subdivision split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NCAA Division I subdivision split")]

The [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") decided to split Division I into two subdivisions in 1978, then called [I-A](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") for larger schools, and [I-AA](/wiki/NCAA_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Football Championship Subdivision") for the smaller ones. The NCAA had devised the split, in part, with the Ivy League in mind, but the conference did not move down for four seasons despite the fact that there were many indications that the ancient eight were on the wrong side of an increasing disparity between the big and small schools. In 1982, the NCAA created a rule that stated a program's average attendance must be at least 15,000 to qualify for I-A membership. This forced the conference's hand, as only some of the member schools met the attendance qualification. Choosing to stay together rather than stand their ground separately in the increasingly competitive I-A subdivision, the Ivy League, along with several other conferences and independent programs moved down into I-AA starting with the 1982 season.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Recent history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/The_Harvard_football_team_takes_the_field.jpg/250px-The_Harvard_football_team_takes_the_field.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Harvard_football_team_takes_the_field.jpg)

Harvard players in 2019

Since the formation of the Ivy League in 1956, Harvard has won outright or shared 20 Ivy League championships (8 outright; 12 shared), 1961 (6–3), 1966 (8–1), 1968 (8–0–1), 1974 (7–2), 1975 (7–2), 1982 (7–3), 1983 (6–2–2), 1987 (8–2), 1997 (9–1), 2001 (9–0), 2004 (10–0), 2007 (8–2), 2008 (9–1), 2011 (9–1), 2013 (9–1), 2014 (10–0), 2015 (9–1), 2023 (8–2), 2024 (8-2), and 2025 (9-1). The Crimson is behind Dartmouth's 21 Ivy League Football Championships.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In summer 2020, the Ivy League announced that the fall season would be postponed because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic");[[35]](#cite_note-35) the 2020 football season was eventually cancelled altogether. Harvard did not resume play until September 2021, after a nearly two-year hiatus, with a 44-9 victory over [Georgetown](/wiki/2021_Georgetown_Hoyas_football_team "2021 Georgetown Hoyas football team").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Yale

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Yale")]

Main article: [Harvard–Yale football rivalry](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry")

Harvard and Yale have been competing against each other in football since 1875. The annual rivalry game between the two schools, known as "[The Game](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry")", is played in November at the end of the football season. As of 2022, Yale led the series 69–61–8. The Game is the second oldest continuing rivalry and also the third most-played [rivalry game](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_college_football_rivalry_games "List of NCAA college football rivalry games") in college football history, after the [Lehigh–Lafayette Rivalry](/wiki/The_Rivalry_(Lafayette%E2%80%93Lehigh) "The Rivalry (Lafayette–Lehigh)") (1884) and the [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")–Yale game (1873). *[Sports Illustrated](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated "Sports Illustrated") On Campus* rated the Harvard–Yale rivalry the sixth-best in college athletics in 2003. [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy") played football for Harvard and caught a touchdown pass in the 1955 Harvard/Yale game. In 2006, Yale ended a five-game losing streak against Harvard, winning 34–13. The star of the game was freshman QB Derrick Szu-tu. Despite never playing high school football, the frosh went 27-for-35 for 359 yards and six passing touchdowns (along with 6 interceptions and 4 lost fumbles). That Harvard winning streak was third longest in the history of the series, after Yale's 1902–1907 six-game winning streak and Yale's 1880–1889 eight-game winning streak. Harvard has since beaten Yale in 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014 and 2015. The Game is significant for historical reasons as the rules of The Game soon were adopted by other schools. Football's rules, conventions, and equipment, as well as elements of "atmosphere" such as the [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") and [fight song](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song"), include many elements pioneered or nurtured at Harvard and Yale.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Harvard Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Harvard Stadium")]

Main article: [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Harvard_Stadium_aerial.jpg/250px-Harvard_Stadium_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_Stadium_aerial.jpg)

[Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium"), November 2008

[Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium") is a horseshoe-shaped [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") in the [Allston](/wiki/Allston,_Massachusetts "Allston, Massachusetts") neighborhood of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), in the United States. The stadium is an important historic landmark. Built in 1903, it is the nation's oldest stadium. Penn's Franklin Field is the oldest site still in use (1895) but its current stadium was built in 1922. It was also the world's first massive reinforced-concrete structure, and considered at the time of construction to be the 'finest structure of its kind in the world'. The structure was completed in just six months, mainly by the efforts of Harvard students, and for a budget of $200,000. Thus 'the stadium represents the thought, the money, the ideas, the planning, and the manual labor of Harvard men'.[[45]](#cite_note-45) As such, it is one of four athletic arenas distinguished as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Boston "List of National Historic Landmarks in Boston") (the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum"), [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(stadium) "Rose Bowl (stadium)") and the [Yale Bowl](/wiki/Yale_Bowl "Yale Bowl") are the other three).[[46]](#cite_note-46) The stadium seats 30,323. Temporary steel stands were added in the stadiums to expand capacity to 57,166 until 1951. Afterward, there were smaller temporary stands until the building of the Murr Center (which is topped by the new scoreboard) in 1998. In 2006, Harvard installed both [FieldTurf](/wiki/FieldTurf "FieldTurf") and lights.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## All-Americans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Crimson_football&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: All-Americans")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Eddie_Mahan.jpg/250px-Eddie_Mahan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Mahan.jpg)

Three-time All-American [Eddie Mahan](/wiki/Eddie_Mahan "Eddie Mahan") was named by [Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe") as the greatest football player of all time.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Hamilton_Fish_III.jpg/250px-Hamilton_Fish_III.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hamilton_Fish_III.jpg)

Two-time All-American [Hamilton Fish III](/wiki/Hamilton_Fish_III "Hamilton Fish III") served 25 years in [Congress](/wiki/U.S._House_of_Representatives "U.S. House of Representatives").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/57/Huntington_Hardwick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huntington_Hardwick.jpg)

[Huntington "Tack" Hardwick](/wiki/Huntington_Hardwick "Huntington Hardwick") was called "a big, fine-looking aristocrat from blue-blood stock" who "loved combat – body contact at crushing force – a fight to the finish."[[72]](#cite_note-72)
